data.title: Nvidia's Jensen Huang Says Customers Will Determine Financing of AI Buildout — WSJ
data.description:
By Sebastian Herrera

Nvidia Chief Executive Jensen Huang said the financing of the costly AI buildout is up to its customers. Investors have grown nervous about the amount of money tech companies are spending to build out artificial-intelligence infrastructure and train AI models.

"We are seeing the opportunity to grow," he said on a call with investors. He added that the cost to compute is declining, and that the company's customer base can rely on it because it has a resilient supply chain.

"Our reputation and our credibility is incredible," he said. "It takes a really strong balance sheet to do that, to support the level of growth and the rate of growth."

He also said Nvidia expects its investment of as much as $100 billion in OpenAI to pay off because it is a "once in a generation company."

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: AI-focused developers help fuel New York City life, city agency chief says
data.description:
By Megan Davies

New York City companies focused on artificial intelligence, which rely on human interaction to grow, are boosting office leasing and inspiring the return of workers to the city, said Andrew Kimball, president and CEO of New York City Economic Development Corporation.

The industry-wide trend started in the San Francisco Bay Area, where major AI developers are based, following the launch of ChatGPT three years ago.

"The percentage of companies that are calling themselves AI companies, no matter what the sector they're in, is just going up, up, up," Kimball said at the Reuters Momentum AI Finance conference in New York on Tuesday.

New York is aiming to attract top talent and businesses in the AI wave, which will boost office leasing and street activity, he added.

"What I hear over and over again, what I've seen with my own eyes ... is, in companies that are AI-focused, they're not talking about being back three or four days a week to the office," said Kimball. "They are in seven days a week. Because that human interaction is so critical to the success of their output."

Kimball addressed the fear that some workers will lose their jobs as AI replaces them in repetitive or data-intensive tasks.

"There's going to certainly be shaking out, there's going to be some jobs lost, there's going to be some jobs that are augmented," said Kimball. "But I think everything I have read and seen, ... is that New York is a net winner (from AI)."

Kimball, who was appointed in February 2022 by New York City Mayor Eric Adams, said he would "love to keep serving the city." But Mayor-elect Zohran Mamdani has yet to announce who will head the EDC.

Mamdani's office did not respond to a request for comment.

data.title: Kulicke & Soffa Shares Rise as 1Q Outlook Tops Estimates on Increased Demand
data.description:
By Kelly Cloonan

Shares of Kulicke & Soffa Industries rose after its outlook for the current quarter topped analyst forecasts as it expects increased demand.

The stock climbed 14% to $40.40 on Wednesday in after-hours trading. Shares are down 24% this year through market close.

The semiconductor assembly technology company on Wednesday forecast adjusted earnings per share of about 33 cents on revenue of $190 million, give or take $10 million, for the fiscal first quarter.

Analysts polled by FactSet project adjusted earnings of 23 cents a share and revenue of $168.8 million.

Lester Wong, the company's interim chief executive and finance chief, said the company is increasingly encouraged by improving end market trends and order activity.

"Our global operations and supply chain teams are preparing for increased customer demand over the coming quarters," Wong said.

The outlook came after adjusted earnings and revenue beat Wall Street's expectations in the company's latest quarter.

The company posted a fiscal fourth-quarter profit of $6.4 million, or 12 cents a share, compared with $12.1 million, or 22 cents a share, a year earlier.

Adjusted earnings per share were 28 cents, ahead of estimates of 22 cents a share, according to analysts polled by FactSet.

Revenue fell to $177.6 million compared with $181.3 million a year ago. Analysts expected $170 million.

Write to Kelly Cloonan at kelly.cloonan@wsj.com

data.title: US authorizes export of advanced American semiconductors to companies in Saudi, UAE
data.description:
The U.S. Commerce Department said on Wednesday it has authorized the export of advanced artificial intelligence chips, the equivalent of up to 35,000 NVIDIA Blackwell chips, to two companies in Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates.

The two companies are G42, which is a state-run AI company based in Abu Dhabi, and Humain, which is a Saudi government-backed AI venture.

"Both companies are receiving approvals to purchase the equivalent of up to 35,000 Nvidia Blackwell chips (GB300s)," the Commerce Department said in a statement. A total of 35,000 Blackwell's are worth an estimated $1 billion, but prices vary.

"The approvals are conditioned on both companies meeting rigorous security and reporting requirements," the Commerce Department said.

Earlier in the day Humain, a government-backed Saudi AI firm, said it planned to purchase of 600,000 Nvidia AI chips by HUMAIN.

HUMAIN and Elon Musk's xAI plan to jointly develop data centers in Saudi Arabia, including a 500 megawatt facility.

data.title: Fitch Revises Transelec´s Outlook to Negative; Affirms IDRs at 'BBB'
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-Rio de Janeiro-19 November 2025:

Fitch Ratings has affirmed the Long-Term Foreign-Currency and Long-Term Local-Currency Issuer Default Ratings (IDRs) of Transelec S.A. (Transelec) at 'BBB', and its National Long-Term Rating at 'AA(cl)'. The Rating Outlook on the corporate ratings has been revised to Negative from Stable.

The Negative Outlook reflects the low leverage headroom, cash outflows, expected sustained negative free cash flow (FCF) across the investment cycle and tighter liquidity position despite committed facilities. Ratings also consider Transelec's low business-risk profile, stable operating cash generation and strategic importance in Chile.

The company remains resilient following database-driven revenue provisions related to 2020-2025 tariffs, with up to CLP130.8 billion (approximately USD135 million) in retroactive regulatory adjustments to be returned to customers. Undrawn committed credit lines of about USD250 million provide flexibility, but headroom is expected to narrow as the capex plan is executed and the extraordinary outflow is met.

Key Rating Drivers

Inventory Adjustment: Transelec identified inconsistencies affecting its regulated asset base under the 2020-2025 tariff cycle and proactively recognized provisions for lower revenue, capped at CLP130.8 billion, with only limited additional accumulation expected. The amount was determined after a review of information voluntarily provided by Transelec, following a self-report filed with the Comisión Nacional de Energía (CNE). Fitch does not expect material incremental provisions, as the total inventory adjustment was fully recognized in advance and is reflected in the June 2025 financial statements.

Operating Performance and Liquidity Operating cash flow totaled CLP115 billion for the 12 months ended June 2025, broadly consistent with Fitch's base case, as the provision is non-cash and working capital remains stable. Liquidity has weakened, with cash of CLP132 billion, supported by undrawn committed credit lines of about USD250 million and no significant debt maturities until December 2027. In Fitch's view, liquidity risk is elevated as the company funds the extraordinary cash outflow and capex. Fitch expects reliance on available lines and internal cash generation, with limited room to increase dividends or debt without pressuring the rating.

Leverage Trend: Despite the provisions, EBITDA leverage was 6.4x for the 12 months ended June 2025, below the 6.5x negative sensitivity. Fitch estimates leverage will decline below 6.0x by December 2025 and remain below 5.5x over the rating horizon, supported by solid operating cash generation from new transmission assets entering service. As leverage trends below 6.0x and toward the mid 5x range, Fitch expects it to be broadly consistent with sector peers, converging toward peer group leverage benchmarks.

Negative Free Cash Flow: Fitch expects negative FCF over the forecast period due to Transelec's capex program, which primarily focuses on new construction. The backlog under construction is close to USD400 million. Historically, Transelec has paid extraordinary dividends while maintaining a minimum cash balance; however, dividend payouts are expected to be reduced to finance capex and address the recent provisions. Negative FCF is expected to constrain internal deleveraging and liquidity headroom.

Liquidity pressured but Manageable: Transelec has a manageable debt maturity profile, with the next significant maturity of CLP530,000 million in December 2027. Total financial debt as of June 2025 was approximately CLP1,901 billion, entirely at fixed rates, with 18% in U.S. dollar-denominated international notes and 82% in domestic bonds denominated in Unidad de Fomento (UF). Fitch expects near-term liquidity to tighten as the company funds regulatory payments and executes its capex backlog. Available cash and USD250 million of undrawn committed facilities provide a cushion, but headroom relative to expected uses has narrowed, underpinning the Negative Outlook.

Low Business-Risk Profile: The rating reflects a low-business-risk profile, with stable, predictable cash flow without exposure to volume or price risk. Chile's stable regulatory framework supports certainty of regulated transmission revenue and returns. Legacy tariffs are revised every four years, with post-tax profitability of 7% indexed to U.S. CPI and local inflation. New assets receive a stable 20-year tariff before entering revenue recognition. The framework allows asset ownership, eliminating concession renewal risk. Transelec is the main high-voltage supplier in Chile, with 57% of National Electric System assets and coverage of about 98% of the population. Fitch expects Transelec to remain a key operator as expansion auctions proceed.

Standalone Credit Profile: Transelec's ratings are derived from the company's creditworthiness on a standalone basis. Fitch believes the company's ultimate shareholders, consisting of three Canadian institutional investors and China Southern Power Grid Co., Ltd. (CSG; A/Satable), will maintain Transelec's historical financial discipline and business strategy related to liquidity, dividends and capital structure.

Peer Analysis

Similar to its peers, Transelec exhibits a low business risk profile and predictable cash flow generation, characteristics of transmission companies operating in a regulated industry. The company's peers include Sociedad de Transmision Austral S.A. (STA; BBB/Stable), Engie Energia Chile S.A. (BBB/Stable) as an integrated utility company, Redeia Corporacion S.A. (Redeia; A-/Stable), Interconexion Electrica S.A. E.S.P. (ISA; BBB/Negative), Consorcio Transmantaro S.A. (CTM; BBB/Stable), Transmissora Alianca de Energia Eletrica S.A. (Taesa; BB+/Stable), and Empresa de Transmision Electrica, S.A. (ETESA; B/Stable).

Transelec's total debt to EBITDA leverage ratio is considered high for its rating category, similar to CTM's and ETESA's leverage ratios. High leverage is offset by the fact that Transelec owns and operates all of its assets, while its regional peers operate under a long-term concession model. Similar to Transelec, Taesa, and ETESA, STA concentrates its operations in the energy transmission business, generating EBITDA margins consistently above 75%.

The main difference between these companies is that Taesa's ratings are negatively affected by Brazil's Country Ceiling of 'BB+'. ISA has a larger scale of operations with geographical diversification in the region. ETESA's ratings reflect its strong linkage with Panama's sovereign (BB+/Stable) due to the government's 100% ownership in the company and history of financial support.

In terms of business risk, Transelec is similar to Redeia, a transmission owner and operator in Spain with predictable regulated revenue, a conservative capital structure, and strong liquidity, which supports Redeia's ratings at two notches above Transelec.

Key Assumptions

-- For existing National and Zonal transmission assets, a four-year tariff review based on the estimated replacement value of the investments, with a profitability of 7% after taxes, indexed to the U.S. CPI and local inflation;

-- New National and Zonal transmission assets under a 20-year stable tariff;

-- Stable tariff for dedicated transmission assets under private agreements with a remaining life of 20 years;

-- EBITDA margins above 80%;

-- Committed expansion and new projects come online during 2025-2028;

-- Total capex for CLP1.320 million during 2025-2028;

-- Dividend payout rate at 30% on the following years to finance the capex plan;

-- Continued refinancing of short-term maturities.

RATING SENSITIVITIES

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade:

-- Sustained leverage above 5.5x total debt/EBITDA ratio;

-- A change in the company's strategy to be more aggressive in its leverage, dividends and capex;

-- A significant adverse change to Chile's regulatory framework;

-- A change of control in Transelec that alters the company's business and financial strategy, dividend distribution, and cash extraction, as well as changes in corporate governance practices.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade:

-- EBITDA leverage consistently below 4.5x would be seen credit positive.

Liquidity and Debt Structure

Transelec's liquidity is supported by good access to refinancing and a comfortable debt maturity schedule. As of June 2025, the company had cash on hand of CLP131.5 billion. In January 2025, the company paid the USD375 million international notes. Liquidity is further buoyed by a USD250 million undrawn committed revolving credit facility, renewed for three years during February 2024. With this the company has a total liquidity of about CLP365.5 billion.

The company's next maturity corresponds to the CLP530.000 million national notes due 2027.

Issuer Profile

Transelec is the largest transmission company in Chile and main supplier of high voltage systems in the country, with 10,049 kilometers of transmission lines and 82 substations. The company owns and operates all of its strategic assets and serves approximately 98% of the county's total population.

The assignment, publication, or dissemination of a rating by Fitch shall not constitute a consent by Fitch to use its name as an expert in connection with any registration statement filed under the United States securities laws, the Financial Services and Markets Act of 2000 of the United Kingdom, or the securities laws of any particular jurisdiction. Due to the relative efficiency of electronic publishing and distribution, Fitch research may be available to electronic subscribers up to three days earlier than to print subscribers.

For Australia, New Zealand, Taiwan and South Korea only: Fitch Australia Pty Ltd holds an Australian financial services license (AFS license no. 337123) which authorizes it to provide credit ratings to wholesale clients only. Credit ratings information published by Fitch is not intended to be used by persons who are retail clients within the meaning of the Corporations Act 2001

Fitch Ratings, Inc. is registered with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission as a Nationally Recognized Statistical Rating Organization (the “NRSRO”). While certain of the NRSRO’s credit rating subsidiaries are listed on Item 3 of Form NRSRO and as such are authorized to issue credit ratings on behalf of the NRSRO (see https://www.fitchratings.com/site/regulatory), other credit rating subsidiaries are not listed on Form NRSRO (the “non-NRSROs”) and therefore credit ratings issued by those subsidiaries are not issued on behalf of the NRSRO. data.title: Ross Stores Announces Quarterly Dividend
data.description:
DUBLIN, Calif.--(BUSINESS WIRE)--November 19, 2025--

Ross Stores, Inc. announced today that the Company's Board of Directors declared a regular quarterly cash dividend of $0.405 per common share, payable on December 31, 2025 to stockholders of record as of December 9, 2025.

About Ross Stores, Inc.

Ross Stores, Inc. is an S&P 500, Fortune 500, and Nasdaq 100 (ROST) company headquartered in Dublin, California, with fiscal 2024 revenues of $21.1 billion. Currently, the Company operates Ross Dress for Less(R) ("Ross"), the largest off-price apparel and home fashion chain in the United States with 1,909 locations in 44 states, the District of Columbia, Guam, and Puerto Rico. Ross offers first-quality, in-season, name brand and designer apparel, accessories, footwear, and home fashions for the entire family at savings of 20% to 60% off department and specialty store regular prices every day. The Company also operates 364 dd's DISCOUNTS(R) stores in 22 states that feature a more moderately-priced assortment of first-quality, in-season, name brand apparel, accessories, footwear, and home fashions for the entire family at savings of 20% to 70% off moderate department and discount store regular prices every day. Additional information is available at www.rossstores.com.

View source version on businesswire.com: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20251119851693/en/

data.title: Japan policymakers agree to watch market with 'strong sense of urgency', yen weakens
data.description:
Key points:
  • BOJ Ueda meets ministers, reaffirms vigilance to market moves
  • Govt, BOJ reaffirm resolve to achieve stable inflation with wage gains
  • Katayama says 'no objection' to BOJ's adjustment of monetary support
  • Meeting comes amid yen falls driven by Takaichi's spending plan
  • Stimulus package could exceed 20 trillion yen, Kyodo reports

By Makiko Yamazaki and Leika Kihara

Bank of Japan Governor Kazuo Ueda and key economic ministers in the country's new government have agreed to watch market developments with a "strong sense of urgency," Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama said on Wednesday.

Katayama also said she had "no particular objection" to Ueda's explanation that the BOJ was gradually adjusting the degree of monetary support through interest rate hikes.

The yen weakened after Katayama spoke, crossing 156 per dollar.

EXPECTATIONS OF STIMULUS WEIGH ON YEN

Wednesday's meeting came at a time when the yen is being driven down on market expectations that Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's new administration will deliver a huge spending package backed by low interest rates.

The Kyodo news agency reported on Wednesday Japan's stimulus package could exceed 20 trillion yen ($129 billion) and be funded by an extra budget of around 17 trillion yen.

"We reaffirmed our readiness to do our utmost to achieve stable inflation accompanied by rising wages, and sustainable economic growth," Katayama told reporters after her meeting with Ueda and Economic Revitalisation Minister Minoru Kiuchi.

"We also reaffirmed that we will watch markets with a strong sense of urgency and pursue careful communication," she said.

GATHERING FOLLOWS MEETING BETWEEN PM AND BOJ GOVERNOR

The gathering followed Ueda's meeting with Takaichi on Tuesday, where the two discussed economic development, monetary policy and currency rate moves, according to the central bank chief.

The last time the BOJ chief met with the finance and economic revitalisation ministers of then premier Shigeru Ishiba was in October last year, when they warned against excessive yen falls and agreed to carefully monitor market developments.

At that gathering, they also reaffirmed that the government and BOJ would work closely together to achieve the bank's 2% inflation target based on a joint agreement signed in 2013.

Katayama said she proposed making a technical tweak to the joint agreement but keeping substantial elements unchanged.

Katayama said the size of the government's spending package has not been decided yet, and that Wednesday's meeting did not elaborate on the issue.

TAKAICHI AN ADVOCATE OF EXPANSIONARY FISCAL, MONETARY POLICY

The inauguration last month of Takaichi, who is known as an advocate of expansionary fiscal and monetary policy, has also complicated the BOJ's efforts to gradually push up still-low interest rates.

After exiting a massive stimulus last year, the BOJ raised interest rates twice including in January. While it has kept rates steady at 0.5% since then, Ueda has signalled the bank's readiness to raise rates again either in December or January.

Speaking to reporters after his meeting with Takaichi, Ueda said the premier did not make any request on monetary policy and appeared to acknowledge his argument that gradual rate hikes will help the economy achieve sustainable, long-term growth.

Under pressure from then-Prime Minister Shinzo Abe to take bolder steps to beat deflation, the BOJ in 2013 signed the joint statement with the government and committed itself to achieve its 2% inflation target "at the earliest date possible."

The statement also calls for the government and BOJ to make efforts to pull Japan out of deflation, language which has been retained despite the world's fourth-largest economy now seeing inflation exceed the central bank's 2% target for well over three years.

Some analysts have criticised the joint statement as outdated and giving politicians an excuse to keep ramping up spending.

($1 = 155.3400 yen)

data.title: Bargain Hunting and Nvidia Results Boost New Zealand Stocks
data.description:
The NZX 50 rose 37 points, or 0.3%, to 13,364 in early Thursday trade, snapping a two-day losing streak.

The rebound tracked a Wall Street rally, fueled by Nvidia’s blowout earnings that lifted US tech stocks in after-hours trading.

Bargain hunting also supported sentiment after the index hit a four-week low on Wednesday.

Meanwhile, the Reserve Bank of New Zealand is expected to cut rates again next week, following three reductions this year amid tepid growth and soft inflation, signaling a continued dovish stance.

However, gains were tempered by caution ahead of October trade data, after September figures showed weak import demand in New Zealand.

Sector-wise, communications, consumer services, and non-energy minerals led the advance.

Notable movers included AFT Pharmaceuticals (4.6%), Vista Group Intl. (2.0%), PGG Wrightson (1.4%), and Spark NZ (1.3%).
